Project Development Objective (PDO)
Original PDO
The project development objective (PDO) is: “to improve budget planning and credibility, financial control and
accountability, and legislative and public oversight in the management and use of government finances in Sierra
Leone”. Through strengthened institutional capacity for the delivery of effective PFM and oversight, the project will
contribute to enhancing fiscal discipline, strategic allocation of resources, and service delivery efficiency.

With this objective, a sound PFM system will be established and nurturedunder the proposed project to contribute
to poverty reduction as: (i) it serves as a prerequisite for long-term and sustainable poverty reduction, enabling the
country to manage its own development; (ii) it helps ensure that budget planning and discipline are compatible with
macroeconomic stability, resource allocation is in line with poverty reduction strategies, activities are implemented
efficiently, and results are followed up; (iii) national policies are transformed into actions through the PFM system,
and services are delivered; and (iv) democratic governance entails democratic control over resources.

In order to achieve the PDO, the Project will support the implementation of priority reform actions identified in the
GoSL approved PFM Reform Strategy (2014-2017) which was developed under the following key themes: (i) Budget
Planning, Comprehensiveness, and Credibility; (ii) Financial Control and Accountability, Service Delivery and
Oversight; (iii) Revenue Mobilization; and (iv) Strengthening Local Governance Financial Management through Local
Councils for Effective Decentralization. It will establish tangible improvements in budget credibility, coverage, and
realism through improvements in macro-fiscal planning and forecasting, budget compilation and
comprehensiveness, it will instill improved internal and external controls and accountability processes in
expenditure management for enhanced service delivery, it will strengthen revenue collection, accounting and
reporting as a basis for improved fiscal management; it will strengthen the capacity of the decentralized service
delivery arm of government (the local councils) to plan and account for the resources transferred to them; and it
will support the overall governance and management platform for coordinating and monitoring the implementation
of not only thePFM reform project but the PFM reform strategy as a whole.

The PFM Reform Strategy objectives against each of the themes have beenaccordingly built in the relevant
components of the proposed project for efficiency and for the sake of operational implementationof the project.
The strategy is anchored on five basic criteria: sequencing of major actions; simplicity and realism; lessons learnedin
prior reforms; ownership and sustainability; and partnership and collaboration.

Project Beneficiaries: The beneficiaries of theProject will include: the Ministry of Finance and economic Planning
(Comptroller and Accountant General’s Department, Budget Bureau, PFM Reforms Unit, Aid and Debt Management
Unit, and Macro-Fiscal Analysis Unit), National Revenue Authority, Audit Service of Serra Leone, the Finance and
Appropriations Committee and the Public Accounts Committee of Parliament, National Public Procurement
Authority, Local Councils. MDAs will also be key stakeholders and beneficiaries of the reforms supported under the
project. In addition, the Project will benefit the non-state actors in executing their demand-side oversight
responsibilities in the area of PFM.

Revised PDO
The modified project development objective reads as follows: to improve the public financial management system
and accountability in the use of government finances.

I. PROJECT CONTEXT AND DEVELOPMENT OBJECTIVES

 A. CONTEXT AT APPRAISAL

   Context
1. Macroeconomic challenges constrain Sierra Leone's development and are characterized by growth volatility and
   dependence on extractives, weak institutions, fragility, and low levels of human capital. Although relatively stable
   politically, Sierra Leone’s economy is one of the most volatile economies in the world with among the weakest
   development outcomes and at project appraisal was classified as an FCV country. Access to health and education
   services was very low. Life expectancy, at 51 years, was one of the lowest. More than 10 percent of newborns died
   before age five; a quarter of children under five were chronically malnourished; and two-thirds of adults had
   incomplete primary education, or none. The economy is undiversified and has been primarily driven by mining and
   agricultural production, both vulnerable to changes in global commodity prices, resulting in high volatility in
   economic growth. After the end of a long and brutal civil war (1991-2002), GDP growth averaged 7.8 percent during
   2003-14. With population growth of 2 percent per year, real per capita GDP growth averaged 5.8 percent. Over the
   period 2010-2014 annual aggregate government revenues grew at an average annual rate of 41.23% from Le
   844,100 (millions) to Le 3,313,749 (millions) and aggregate government expenditure grew at an average annual rate
   of 23.4% from Le 1,138,339 (millions) to 2,628,249 (millions). Fragility is a major issue with vulnerability to the
   impact of natural disasters, climate related shocks as well as macroeconomic shocks worsened by pre-existing
   vulnerabilities resulting from a youth demographic that has low levels of education and poor employment
   opportunities as well as power asymmetry, elite capture, and low institutional capacity. Increases in gas prices in
   2011 led to increased Government borrowing and higher inflation. In this context, Public Financial Management
   (PFM) reform is important to a) improve fiscal discipline, b) allocate resources to priority needs, and c) improve the
   delivery of public services through economic, efficient, and effective use of public finances.

2. PFM legal framework and systems had improved, however, there remained several areas where improvements
   are required. Strengthened PFM legal and regulatory framework in budgeting, accountability and procurement had
   contributed to improvements, supported by the implementation of Government’s Financial Management
   Information System in the Accountant General’s office since 2005 with roll-out to several Ministries, Departments
   and Agencies (MDAs). However, the Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability assessments (PEFA) reveal that
   overall, the PFM system is weak and in need of significant improvements. Challenges remain in key areas including
   budget credibility and predictability, fiscal management, expenditure control, low levels of transparency, incomplete
   collection and reporting of revenues from natural resources, lacking cash management systems, off-budget
   expenditures and a budget system that is focused on inputs rather than results. PFM institutional capacity is low and
   budgets to finance development, particularly in accountability institutions, remains constrained by the fiscal
   situation.

3. The Bank’s rationale for supporting financing of PFM reforms was clear and the proposed reforms aligned well
   with the National agenda for prosperity 2013-2018. The Bank had been a lead co-financier of PFM reforms
    around the time of appraisal and the period leading up and was a key partner in providing technical
    assistance to PFM reforms. The project succeeded the Integrated Public Financial Management Reform

7. The references in the PDO Indicator description are to the respective indicator in the PEFA methodology. The
   baselines for 1-7 are contained in the 2013 PEFA report. The PEFA methodology, as a widely used PFM diagnostic
   across the globe, which is a comprehensive measure of performance in PFM, was considered useful as a benchmark
   and to measure the results of the project.

   Components
8. Component 1: Enhancing Budget Planning and Credibility: The objectives of this component was to: strengthen the
   macro fiscal forecasting and public investment functions of the government to improve overall budget planning and
   strengthen systems and procedures for budget formulation; and build capacity for managing contingent liabilities.
   Sub-component 1.1: Strengthening budgetary and macro-fiscal planning & forecasting. This sub-component aimed at
   building procedures, systems, and capacity to improve the planning phase of the medium-term budget system. Sub-
   component 1.2: Supporting the Public Investment Management Function: This sub-component aimed at improving
   the preparation and prioritization of the public investment program by supporting the establishment of the new
   Project Investment Management Unit (PIMU) through its initial year(s) of operation. Sub-component 1.3: Fiscal Risk
   Management and Reporting: This component aimed to establish a framework for effectively managing government’s
   fiscal risks. Sub-Component 1.4: Strengthening Budget Framework and Formulation: The objective of this sub-
   component was to facilitate transition to medium-term budgeting by supporting the government to address key
   systems and resource management issues.

9. Component 2: Financial Control, Accountability and Oversight: The objective was to strengthen financial control in
   the government, improve accountability systems and practices, and enhance independent and public oversight in the
   management. Sub-component 2.1: Strengthening financial systems, controls, accounting and reporting: The
   objective of this sub-component was to strengthen the government’s financial management system, improve
   accountability and reporting, thus contributing to more efficient use of public resources for service delivery. The sub-


                                                                                                               Page 9 of 65
       The World Bank
      Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



    component entailed implementation of activities under three key areas: (i) Integrated Financial Management
    Information System (IFMIS); (ii) Accounting and Reporting; and (iii) Internal Audit. Sub-component 2.2: Cash, Debt
    and Aid Management: This sub-component aimed at improving fiscal control through implementing Treasury Single
    Account and strengthening regulatory/operational framework for managing public debt and aid. Sub-component 2.3:
    Strengthening Public Procurement and Contract Management Systems: The objective of the sub-component was to
    strengthen institutional capacity of the procurement function and enhance contract management systems across
    government. Sub-component 2.4: Strengthening External Audit: The objectives of this sub-component were to
    support strengthening of financial oversight role of Audit Service of Sierra Leone (ASSL) and improve co-ordination
    with other parts of the government. Sub-component 2.5: Strengthening Legislative and Public Oversight: To reinforce
    accountability and use of public finances through strengthened oversight functions.

10. Component 3: Supporting the Strengthening of Revenue Mobilization and Administration Systems: The component
    aimed to strengthen revenue policy and oversight of revenue collection; improve the efficiency and integrity of
    revenue administration to increase domestic revenue; and integrate revenue systems with overall PFM system. Sub-
    component 3.1: Tax Legislation and Regulatory Framework and Policy: The objective of this sub-component was to
    strengthen legislative and regulatory functions. Sub-component 3.2 Tax Systems, business processes, collection, and
    reconciliation: The objective of this sub-component was to strengthen systems that enhanced the efficiency in tax
    administration and non-tax revenues.

11. Component 4: Strengthening Local Governance (LG), financial management and accountability systems: The
    objective of this component was to improve financial management systems in LCs and strengthen accountability and
    oversight institutions. Sub-component 4.1: Strengthening Budgetary Planning Systems: The objective of this sub-
    component was to ensure service delivery by LG to citizens was in line with the Agenda for Prosperity (AfP) goals and
    coordination between LCs and MDAs was optimized. Sub-component 4.2: Strengthening Budget Execution,
    Accounting and Reporting Systems: The objective of this sub-component was to strengthen LG PFM systems. Sub-
    Component 4.3: Local Governance - Strengthening Public Procurement Systems: The objective was to strengthen the
    public procurement systems at LG level. Sub-Component 4.4: Strengthening Oversight and Transparency Systems
    and Practices: The objective of this sub-component was to ensure that LCs were transparent and accountable in the
    delivery of services.

12. Component 5: PFM Reform Coordination and Project Management: The objective of this component was to provide
    a continuing institutional and coordination basis for overseeing the implementation of the PFM Reform Strategy.
    Sub-Component 5.1: Project Management and Coordination: The objective of this sub-component was to provide
    the administrative and operational structure for the seamless management and coordination of the project. Sub-
    Component 5.2: PFM Reforms Directorate: The objective of this sub-component was to provide technical leadership
    in articulating, guiding, and monitoring the overall PFM reforms in Sierra Leone, coordinate and provide an enabling
    platform for building consensus on priority reforms in support of achievement of the overarching PFM
    improvements in Sierra Leone within the spirit of the government's PFM reform strategy and as defined in the
    Agenda for Prosperity.



 B. SIGNIFICANT CHANGES DURING IMPLEMENTATION (IF APPLICABLE)


                                                                                                            Page 10 of 65
       The World Bank
      Public Financial Management Improvement and Consolidation Project (P133424)



   Revised PDOs and Outcome Targets


13. In May 2017 the project was restructured for the first time and as a result the PDO, components and results
    framework were revised, and the closing date extended from 31 March 2018 to 31 March 2020. This first
    restructuring was driven by a) a request by Government to reprioritize PFM reform activities, and b) a project
    financing gap. The Ebola epidemic, during 2014 and 2015, had not only caused project delays- it had also
    highlighted weaknesses in PFM. A report issued by the Auditor General “Report on the Audit of the Management of
    the Ebola Funds” highlighted several important recommendations which contributed to a reconsideration of reform
    priorities. Government requested that the project activities be reprioritized to better respond to new PFM priorities
    and certain activities were dropped (public investment management, fiscal risk management and reporting, cash,
    debt and aide management and local government) and new activities were picked up (e-Procurement and open and
    big data). Ebola had also caused delays and cost increases, for example, preparations for procuring the new
    Integrated Tax Administration System (ITAS) took longer than expected and led to cost increases. Furthermore, the
    withdrawal of DFID and the EU strained overall project finances. At the time of withdrawing DFID funds transferred
    to the MDTF amounted to $4.6m whereas the EU had not released any funds. There had been an assumption that a
    further $1.64m would be released to the MDTF by DFID, but it appears that the amount was not actually
    transferred. DFID’s withdrawal was cited as being due to changes in priorities and focus, whereas with the EU the
    team cited the moratorium that existed at the time regarding the Bank and the EU signing new Administration
    Agreements which eventually caused funds earmarked to be deployed elsewhere.

14. The original project financing was planned at $28.5m and following the restructuring in May 2017 the financing plan
    was revised to $31.7m, an increase of $3.2m. The net financing need of new activities (i.e., after reducing for cost
    of dropped activities) was approximately $3.0m and the remaining $0.2m was the net increase in financing of cost
    increases (i.e., after reallocating funds between components). The table below presents the project financing profile
    before and after the first restructuring.

15. The original PDO (to improve budget planning and credibility, financial control, accountability and oversight in
    government finances in Sierra Leone) was modified to: ‘improve public PFM system and accountability in the use of
    government finances. This was to allow for better definition of project’s strategic objective to represent the
    ultimate impact envisioned for the activities financed.

16. Almost all the PDO level indicators were changed, however, it is noted that the main rationale for the change was to
    move away from the Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability (PEFA)-based indicators and allow more

frequent assessment of project implementation since PEFA assessments may only occur once each 3–4-year period.
The outcome areas reflected in the PDO indicators and Intermediate Indicators remained essentially the same with
modifications made to reflect restructuring at the midterm review (MTR) and the resulting changes in resourced
activities whereas the overall outcome sought remained the enhancement to the overall PFM System. The most
significant changes to the project activities as part of the restructuring at MTR were a) to scale-up revenue
management systems, b) introduce e-Procurement, and c) strengthen citizen engagement through open data. All of
which were consistent with the original PDO and the revised one.

   Rationale for Changes and Their Implication on the Original Theory of Change


22. The Mid-Term-Review (MTR) mission, in October 2016, noted that while the original project design consisted of
    five components and twenty-five sub-components, some of which were allocated only notional amounts that did
    not truly contribute to the attainment of project intermediate outcomes and the overall project objectives . The
    MTR mission further noted that some sub-components were necessarily meant to be activities within the larger sub-
    components, and yet they were accorded the sub-component level. Government agreed to collapse and rename some
    of the sub-components so that the number of stakeholders and project description outlook was not cluttered thus
    helping enhance better implementation monitoring and coordination to ensure achievement of objectives. The MTR
    also noted that there was no meaningful progress on the commitment from the EU to provide a further US$1.81
    million, as was envisioned during the project design. The DFID too indicated that it will not release the remaining
    tranches, which amounted to US$4.95 million, according to the Administration Agreement because of changes in its
    priorities and focus. This restructuring and the AF did not alter the project’s scope but resulted in the revision of
    component descriptions; the associated costs; and the revision of the PDO to better align it with the new component
    structure.

23. Changes to the project following the restructuring, post-MTR, reflected the different source of finance and a
    revision of priority activities to maximize impact on achievement of the project’s objective. Following the
    withdrawal of the EU and FCDO from the project, the Bank provided AF to address the finance gap. This meant a
    different set of priorities could be reflected in the project, and new activities were added (e-Procurement and Open
    Data) and others removed (see below) accordingly. While the PDO was reworded, it rephrased what was essentially
    the same scope (i.e., PFM in Sierra Leone) describing the objective in terms more aligned with the resulting two
    components (i.e., whereas the prior PDO was more aligned with core elements of the annual budget cycle-“PFM”-
    which reflected the component structure).

II. OUTCOME

  A. RELEVANCE OF PDOs

    Assessment of Relevance of PDOs and Rating

 24. The PDO is as relevant today as it was during appraisal i.e.: government’s commitment to improve PFM system in
     the use of government finances. The project was consistent with the Country Assistance Strategy and with the
     government’s Pillar 7 - 'Governance and Public Sector Reform' of the Agenda for Prosperity (2013- 2018), which
     focused on capacity building of State and Non-State institutions; intra- and inter-MDA coordination; and delivery of
     quality services in a transparent manner. The PDO was consistent with the foundational pillar of the World Bank's
     Strategy for Africa: governance and public sector; and fitted well under the IDA-16 policy framework for fragile
     states6. The relevance of deepening PFM reforms cannot be overemphasized for Sierra Leone, which has emerged
     from a post-conflict fragile status.


  B. ACHIEVEMENT OF PDOs (EFFICACY)

    Assessment of Achievement of Each Objective/Outcome

25. The revised PDO specifies two project outcomes: i) to improve the public financial management system, and ii) to
    improve accountability in the use of government finances.

26. The project’s results and achievements were assessed mainly based on data collected through regular project
    progress reports, quarterly M&E reports, the information collected for ISR reporting and the Public Accountability
    and Financial Accountability (PEFA) assessments undertaken in 2018 and 2021. Although several original PDO
    indicators were performance indicators measured as part of PEFA assessments they were removed from the set of
    PDO indicators following the MTR restructuring since they were not assessed frequently. Nevertheless, the PEFA
    methodology is highly reputed among PFM professionals and includes a thorough diagnosis of the key performance
    indicators across the PFM annual budget cycle. Therefore, the PEFA assessment results were considered helpful to
    assess whether the results achieved in the project impacted this broader assessment.

27. The project achieved most of its performance targets7. The project met or exceeded three of the five PDO
    Indicators. The project’s last ISR had reflected that 4 of 5 PDO indicators were met, however, one was based on an
    estimate which was not eventually met (timing of published PAC reports-see below under outcome (ii)). The other
    PDO indicator not met in procurement related to increasing competitive procurement and while the indicator was
    not met it was close, and overall, the change from the baseline was significant. There were eight Intermediate
    indicators of which four (4) were achieved or exceeded and one (1) was partially achieved. The Intermediate
    Indicator on daily revenue reports interfaced with IFMIS system was partially achieved. The project put in place daily
    revenue reports which are automatically generated from NRA systems. However, interface with IFMIS was not

    achieved due to delayed upgrade of the IFMIS system8. Three (3) IRIs were not met in budget credibility, resolution
    of audit issues and the number of trained public procurement professional who are female. The results achieved as
    indicated in the PDO Indicators and the Intermediate Indicators suggest that the project was more effective in
    improving the public financial management system compared with improving accountability in the use of
    government finances.

28. The original and revised PDO were both broad in scope and essentially covered improvement to the PFM annual
    budget cycle cross-sectoral and both at a national and sub-national level, which comprises a very broad and
    complex set of systems. The connection between the initial PDO and the component parts of the annual budget
    cycle were quite explicit, the revised PDO referred to improving the “PFM system” and accountability in the use of
    public expenditure. The ‘broadness of scope’ explicit in the original PDO equally applied in the revised (i.e. PDO did
    not identify targeting aspects of the PFM annual budget cycle to improve through the addition of technologies post-
    MTR). Therefore, the approach to assessing the achievement of the project’s objectives is to focus on the PDO
    Indicators in the first instance but also to reflect on how achievements in these targeted areas may have directly or
    pervasively improved the PFM annual budget cycle and, therefore, the assessment of efficacy is structured using the
    components of the PFM annual budget cycle.

    Outcome (i): To improve the public financial management system

29. The investment to support the improvement of the public financial management system was largely focused on
    leveraging new technologies and scaling up modernized systems covering approximately two thirds of the
    project’s cost. The IFMIS system, which is the core financial management system for the Government of Sierra
    Leone, was eventually rolled out to 61 MDAs from a baseline of 12. Revenue management systems were
    modernized, including implementation of ITAS (to manage domestic revenues) and ASYCUDA World
    implementation in stations that generate over 98% (total customs revenue). The e-Procurement system was not
    installed as initially planned; important preparations were achieved which will make for more effective subsequent
    implementation. Several other important systems enhancements were supported including the development of
    business process requirements for the implementation of an Electronic Document Management System and
    Electronic Funds Transfer with Bank of Sierra Leone.

30. Strengthening budget Systems was evidenced by the attainment of the PDO indicator “the average time taken for
    quarterly budget release to MDAs within the start of the quarter”. This PDO Indicator had a baseline of 5 weeks,
    and a target of 2 weeks which was achieved from around the mid-point of the project and was sustained. This was
    a significant improvement and critical to ensure that spending units had more predictability in accessing the
    resources needed in a timely manner to deliver important public services.

31. Improvements in budget classification were evidenced by improvement in the respective PEFA Indicator. The
    baseline PEFA Indicator “Classification of the budget” was “C” and by 2018 this had improved to “A” and remained
    such in the draft 2022 PEFA assessment. The improvement was grounded on the change in standards from GFSM
    2001 to GFSM 2014 and was further supported by new IFMIS budget module functionality and training. Such

    improvements are important for more effective service delivery and a more effective allocation of resources.
    Budget classification improved at both central and local government levels.

32. The budget process was further enhanced by the improvements in the macro-fiscal planning. The project
    supported the collection and collation of key macroeconomic data to enable the Government to update the
    macroeconomic database. Furthermore, support to the Macro-Fiscal Steering Committee helped produce credible
    and consistent medium-term macro fiscal projections using the Sierra Leone Integrated Macro-Economic Model
    (SLIMM) and the Mineral Forecasting Model. The improvements were evidenced by the increases in the PEFA
    Indicator “Macroeconomic and fiscal forecasting” which went from “C” in 2018 to “B” in the draft 2021 PEFA
    assessment which noted the preparation of budget year forecasts and the two following years and the comparison
    of outcomes with the projections.

33. There is evidence that improved budget systems modestly increased the credibility of the budget. The PEFA
    performance PI-1 performance indicator on expenditure outturn is an important means for assessing budget
    credibility and was initially reflected on the project as a PDO indicator but was revised to be an Intermediate
    indicator after the MTR (“Ratio between actual primary expenditure and original budgeted primary expenditure”)
    apparently because the PEFA assessments are not conducted frequently enough. The baseline for the revised
    intermediate indicator was 121% actual expenditure versus budget with a target of 107%, the target was not met as
    the 2020 aggregate budget outturn was 114% (although it is noted that 2018 was 85% and 2019 was 94%). The
    PEFA assessment reports reflect “slight improvements” for this indicator between 2014 and 2018 and no change
    between 2018 and 2021. The table below presents the expenditure outturns for 2012-2020.

34. Improvements in budgeting systems are yet to translate to significant enhancements to budget credibility. While
    the aggregate expenditure outturn has been reasonable, it is yet to include development loan and credit
    expenditures and variability in the composition of expenditure remains high with excessive budget reallocations.
    Although the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic likely created evolving needs for public expenditures, budget
    credibility remains an ongoing issue for Government. It Is also noted that PEFA indicator of “Budget preparation
    process” declined from “B” to “C” between 2018 and 2021, since the budget call circular (BCC) ceilings were being
    approved by Cabinet before the BCC was issued, whereas currently ceilings are approved by Cabinet after the
    budget proposals have been completed by MDAs.

35. Strengthening revenue management systems has contributed to increases in customs revenues. The project’s
    related PDO indicator “increase in customs revenues paid into the consolidated fund, adjusted for inflation” from a
    baseline of 20% and targeting 50% was achieved with actual out turns of 110% by the end of the project. This was
    particularly impressive given the negative impact of COVID-19 from early 2020. Supporting this, and after passing
    the project-supported regulations for e-customs processing, was the upgrade from ASYCUDA ++ to ASYCUDA World

36. Another key area of support in Domestic Resource Mobilization to the National Revenue Authority was the
    implementation of a ITAS system. The installation of the new ITAS system was subject to significant delays and was
    not contracted until December 2018. Implementation involved three phases, planning and platform preparation
    and integration, rollout core modules11 and roll out other management modules12. By the close of the project all
    modules had achieved user acceptance and the installation was operationally accepted. As at the date of this ICR
    ITAS has availed 24X7 online service delivery to taxpayers, replaced physical and manual compliance requirements13
    with online portal facilitated services, with instant feedback to taxpayers. This reduced staff to taxpayer physical
    interface, reduced costs of travel by taxpayers to NRA offices and reliance on manual documents. The project has
    also improved revenue accounting by enabling NRA systems to interface with 13 banks, automatic update of
    taxpayer ledgers and availed daily online revenue reports. The daily revenue reports can be leveraged by
    Accountant General’s office to improve government’s revenue reporting and cash forecasting/management. Staff
    and taxpayer capacity was enhanced through the support of the project14.. Given the fact that ITAS system was
    commissioned at the end of the project, the impact is yet to be fully seen, however, the completion of the
    installation in light of significant delays by the project’s closure and usage as evidenced by system generated
    statistics are in themselves significant achievements.

39. Whilst an end-to-end e-Procurement system was unable to be achieved as planned under the project import

    Procurement Act already provides for e-Procurement, Public Procurement Regulations were updated in late 2019 to
    ensure legal coverage and mandating of the electronic procurement in GoSL’s legal framework. A cost benefit

40. Procurement price norms established since 2019 have led to significant savings and more competitive
    procurement is happening. In terms of general procurement, legal amendments were made to the Procurement

    $7.7m between Q1, 2019 and Q3, 2021. A public procurement capacity building strategy and curriculum have also
    been completed and training was provided to over 100 public procurement practitioners. The preparation of
    standard bidding documents for specialized procurements (health, ICT and management services) and the
    development of a contract management database with security and audit trail features has helped to reduce the
    amount of non-competitive, as indicated below, and helped with contract management- although this latter area

43. Cash management through Treasury Single Accounts (TSA), Public Investment Management (PIM) and Local
    Council budgeting and accountability received relatively minor project support prior to MTR and were dropped
    following the restructuring of the project in 2017. As of 2021 16 MDAs are within the TSA system, and this is
    helping cash management, while the project did not play a significant role in this progress, it did provide some initial
    training and support. For PIM, the project supported the establishment of the Public Investment Management
    Division with equipment and training and the development of the Public Investment Operation Manuel which is
    called for by the PFM Regulations 2018. For Local Councils, support was provided to build capacity for planning and

    PETRA accounting system and subsequently a needs assessment for the implementation of the PFM Smart

    outcomes; the local councils will receive support under the new Accountable Governance for Basic Service Delivery
    project and pick-up from where this project left off.

    Outcome (ii): Improving accountability in the use of government finances

44. The approach taken to improve the accountability in the use of government finances was to strengthen capacity
    to implement and enforce the oversight reforms contained in the PFM Act 2016 and the PFM Regulations 2018
    and increase citizen engagement in holding Government accountable for the use of public finances. The project
    supported capacity building of internal auditors and supported the establishment of audit committees15 at 15 MDAs
    to provide independence to internal auditors and follow-up on the implementation of internal audit
    recommendations. Support was provided to the Audit Service Sierra Leone in undertaking performance audits. A

    implemented, preparation for an e-Procurement system has been advanced by the project and will be further

  C. EFFICIENCY

    Assessment of Efficiency and Rating

60. While the economic gains already captured and expected to flow from investment under the project are
    satisfactory, the implementation of the project encountered significant delays and the overall efficiency rating
    has been assessed as “Modest” accordingly. The benefits attributable to the results of the project are difficult to
    translate into economic inflows, however, these are estimated as supporting an efficient use of finances. On the
    other hand, delays in the implementation of the project were significant and as such project management costs
    were higher.

61. Despite the challenges in undertaking economic analysis of PFM reform projects, net benefits, and the cost of
    these are considered adequate. PFM reforms can lead to enhanced domestic revenue mobilization and
    effectiveness and efficiency in public expenditures and marginal economic gains early in reforming undeveloped
    PFM systems can be high and relatively less expensive than later marginal gains. However, complexities and
    significant variables including those associated with macro-economic factors make it difficult to isolate economic
    inflows associated with the investment. The PAD assessed economic viability through an NPV analysis of a part of
    the project including teacher emoluments, interest charges on ‘Ways and Means’ advances, and increases in
    revenues which showed a NPV of $11.6m. At the MTR, with the increased emphasis on revenue management
    systems (i.e., ITAS and ASYCUDA World) and associated revenue gains and transaction cost savings and gains from
    e-Procurement, the economic benefits were assessed as justifying the costs. The gains in customs revenues, the
    potential flows from improved indirect and direct taxation administration and the benefits from institutionalizing
    price norms in procurement, which Government has assessed as saving USD$7.7m since the beginning of 2019, will
    over time exceed the costs of the project without taking account of the ‘softer’ gains from improving budget,
    financial and oversight processes.

62. The costs related to financial and revenue management systems accounted for almost two-thirds of the project
    and were considered broadly comparable with similar systems enhancements on other projects. Systems roll-

    outs amounted to approximately $17.3m USD or 60% of the project costs overall. The costs of IFMIS roll-out and
    ITAS implementation can be difficult to compare with other projects given the requirements may be different,
    however some comparative analysis was performed with the Liberia-Public Financial Management Reforms for
    Institutional Strengthening Project (P165000) which is currently under implementation. This project had similar
    objectives and approaches in respect to tax administration and IFMIS roll-out. The budgeted costs for “IFMIS
    Improvement and Rollout Support” amounts to $2.6m and $7.0m for “Capacity Development & Strengthening the
    Technological Basis for Tax Administration”. The IFMIS rollout cost under PFMICP was $6.9m and for the Liberian
    project the budget is $2.6m (or $3.7m in equivalent terms since it involves less MDAs). The difference appears to be
    explained by the starting point for the Liberian project being more amenable to the planned expansion, and it is also
    possible that some of the delays incurred under PFMICP increased the cost. For ITAS, the PFMICP project combined
    the costs of ASYCUDA World and ITAS implementations, however, the contract cost for installation of ITAS was
    $5.7m which seems reasonably comparable to the Liberian project. Delays in ITAS installation were subject to no-
    cost extensions which helped contain costs.

63. A variety of significant implementation delays, some of which were within the control of the project’s
    management and some of which were not, have substantially impaired project efficiency. The implementation
    efficiency of the project was hampered by implementation delays mainly relating to procurement processes. The
    project was subject to three no-cost extensions to ensure the new systems were properly embedded, the first
    extended the project to 31 March 2020, the second was a one-year extension to 31 March 2021 and finally the
    project was extended for a further six months to 30 September 2021. The first no-cost extension was provided to
    afford time to implement new activities resulting from the first restructuring at MTR, the second and third were
    cited as being mostly related to COVID-19.

64. The roll-out of IFMIS to the planned 54 MDAs was not achieved until mid-2019 representing around two years of
    delay. The IFMIS rollout was hampered by delays in contracting a project manager and embedding the
    recommended governance structure and practices. Various technical challenges were encountered including delays
    in setting up the supporting IT infrastructure and migrating MDAs onto the Government network.

65. The ITAS system implementation which was expected to be completed before 30 March 2020 was delayed by a
    year and a half. The initial procurement of the ITAS system was cancelled due to lapses in the procurement process
    and the contract was not signed until October 2018 following a new procurement. Delivery of the ITAS system so
    late in the project has increased the risk that expected gains may not materialize since post-project optimization
    (e.g., troubleshooting and process training) is not adequately managed and financed.

66. The e-Procurement system implementation did not materialize under the project as initially planned and will be
    undertaken under the new Accountable Governance for Basic Service Delivery Project. Although a consultant was
    hired under the project to develop a roadmap for e-Procurement implementation in January 2018, delays in
    establishing a functional e-GP Directorate and defining the requirements of the e-Procurement system meant that
    the original objective could not be delivered within the project’s timeframe, despite the project being effective for
    seven years, and the focus subsequently changed to preparing for procurement of the system under the subsequent
    project.

III. KEY FACTORS THAT AFFECTED IMPLEMENTATION AND OUTCOME

 A. KEY FACTORS DURING PREPARATION

       76. The project preparation benefited from an ongoing PEFA Assessment which helped to identify key
           priorities for PFM reform support. The PEFA Assessment of 2014 was in-process during preparation,
           and this supported defining priorities and shaping the results framework for both the Government
           and the Bank.

       77. The predecessor project “Integrated Public Financial Management Reform Project” closed in 2014
           and the results of this project also helped with the preparation of the project. The predecessor
           project informed both Government and the Bank in preparing the successor project.


 B. KEY FACTORS DURING IMPLEMENTATION

       78. Some of the key factors that contributed to the delay in implementation of project activities
           included:

              a. Complexity of Project Design: The project was designed with five components and twenty-
                 five sub-components. Inclusion of some of the sub-components was based not on objectives
                 but the competing interests of the stakeholders. This structural complexity complicated
                 coordination and implementation of activities on the client side and tracking of progress
                 through monthly reports on the donor side.

              b. Project Effectiveness delay: Effectiveness date was pushed forward by almost nine months
                 to 19 September 2014, owing to the government’s inability to meet some of the core
                 requirements, such as the hiring of an International Procurement Specialist.

              c. Macroeconomic Challenges: Consumer price inflation continues to increase overall driven by
                 commodity price volatilities and the local currency, the Sierra Leonean Leone, continues to
                 depreciate particularly against the US Dollar. The Ebola epidemic and COVID-19 pandemic
                 have increased Government’s expenditure and the cost of servicing debt is crowding-out
                 expenditures in important service delivery areas. The economy continues to be dependent
                 on extractives and in need of economic diversification.

              d. Ebola outbreak (2014-2016): The outbreak took place immediately after project
                 effectiveness (September 2014) and caused about nine months implementation delay. Cost
                 over-run: The Integrated Taxation Administration System (ITAS) was expected to be
                 undertaken earlier on within the project; however, the onset of Ebola took away the
                 preparatory time and resulted in increased cost of the system which could not be fully met
                 through project cost savings (as envisaged). This resulted in the cancellation of the initial

                   ITAS procurement, its re-launch, and further delays in processing approval of the ITAS
                   Project Plan.

              e. Change of government (in May 2018): The new administration caused delays in accepting
                 some of the project activities (for example the E-procurement implementation).

              f.   Withdrawal of committed funds: The MDTF was anticipated to provide US$13m in funding
                   but with the withdrawal of the EU and FCDO the funding achieved was only US$4,6m. The
                   unexpected withdrawal of the two development partners in providing committed funds
                   created financing gap which was resolved through AF following the project’s restructuring at

              g. Change of Administration in early 2018: With a change in Administration in April 2018 the
                 project experienced some delays as the remaining project activities were subject to scrutiny
                 and it took time for acceptance. The e-Procurement initiative was especially delayed

              h. COVID 19 outbreak: Activities were put on hold during nationwide lockdown due to COVID-

              i.   Technical capacity to manage significant PFM reform was constrained despite the project’s

IV. BANK PERFORMANCE, COMPLIANCE ISSUES, AND RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME

 A. QUALITY OF MONITORING AND EVALUATION (M&E)

   M&E Design

80. The progress towards the PDO was monitored through the PDO level and intermediate level results indicators, as
    presented in Annex 1 of the Project Appraisal Document, and revised during project restructurings in 2017, 2019
    and 2021 and was conducted on a continuous basis under the leadership coordination of the PMU (MoF). The full-
    time M&E Specialist under the PMU oversaw coordination, soliciting and deriving the primary data and information
    to report on project progress against the agreed upon baselines, annual targets, frequency of data collections, data
    source and methodology, by responsible institutions for data collection for all indicators.

81. The PDO and intermediate indicators were changed through the project’s implementation and broadly reflected
    progress toward the achievement of the project’s development objective, although not all indicators were a good
    measure of results. The quality of the results framework was reasonable overall but did not always reflect
    performance toward achieving the development objective. For example, the PDO Indicator on the timeliness of
    submission of the PAC report to Parliament did not reflect the work done by the PAC in resolving audit
    recommendations made by the Auditor General which remains a significant issue. Also, the indicator on the ratio
    between “actual primary expenditure and original budgeted primary expenditure” did not account for budget
    composition variances which remain an issue in Sierra Leone.


   M&E Implementation


82. Reviews of implementation progress were undertaken regularly on a monthly, quarterly, and annual basis and
    the results were used to discuss issues and bottlenecks that emerged from time-to-time impeding achievement of
    targeted outcomes. The issues raised were also discussed by project management and the Bank implementation
    support missions and resulting recommendations became action points for implementation follow-up and
    subsequent implementation support. The government's PFM Reforms Steering Committee regularly received and
    reviewed the strategic information on implementation progress from the PMU and the PFMRU, in respect of the
    entire PFM reforms program in the country and provided strategic guidance to enable the project to achieve its
    development objectives. Trained staff was engaged to collect and analyze field data while the PMU oversaw the
    overall progress and provided timely information to assist in the effective decision-making process. The M&E unit
    regularly sent to the World Bank detailed progress reports with up-to-date highlights of the reporting period. The
    Bank teams in turn provided continuous guidance to enable components move forward. Reporting templates were
    developed and used for reporting that captured necessary information from each component.

83. Progress reporting during the first half of the project required improvement to be more comprehensive by being
    more focused on results reporting rather than activities. Training was provided to focal persons at the
    implementing partners to assist them with how to manage results. More focus on allocating project resources to
    support M&E helped to operationalize the results framework, but overall, the quality of reporting, while adequate,
    could have been more results driven. The monitoring of the ITAS implementation was hampered by a lack of clear

B. ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL, AND FIDUCIARY COMPLIANCE

       86. Environmental Safeguards: As a Category 'C' Technical Assistance project, which by its nature does
           not have any environmental safeguard issues. None of Bank's environmental safeguard policies
           were triggered, either at project appraisal stage, or during Additional Financing. The environmental
           category remained at ‘C’.

       87. Social Safeguards: Given the focus on improving budget planning and financial control and
           accountability, the project did not trigger any social safeguards policies. In fact, the social impacts of
           the project are expected to be positive, as it will increase the timely disclosure of overall
           government revenue collections, improve the link between procurement and budget, and improve
           procurement efficiency, all leading to greater accountability. The project was processed under
           safeguards and not ESF.

       88. Fiduciary: The project’s financial management risk was rated as Substantial at appraisal. The risk
           was mitigated by the establishment of a Project Coordinating Unit manned by professionally
           recruited and qualified personnel to handle the day-to-day project management for the
           Government of Sierra Leone and close supervision by the Bank’s FM Specialist as well as provision of
           appropriate support to the Project Finance Team Members. Most of the time the audits were
           undertaken, and interim financial reports prepared in a relatively timely manner. Compliance with
           provisions of the operational manual as well as other positive FM actions had the project maintain
           an ISR rating of satisfactory for most part of the project. The PMU Finance Unit, headed by a
           Qualified Accountant maintained an adequate financial management system in compliance with the
           World Bank’s Financing Agreement and/or other relevant legal documents to support the
           deployment of project resources in an economic, efficient, and effective manner to achieve the
           desired objective. Fixed assets register was maintained and updated subjected to regular
           verification of assets’ location and condition. The project was able to ensure adequate liquidity by
           preparing quarterly forecasts and submit Withdrawal Applications for replenishment of funds to the
           Designated Account. The Special Accounts were reconciled periodically, and timely bank
           reconciliation statements were produced. Reviews including midterm and in-depth reviews by the
           World Bank were conducted with relatively few reported issues.

 C. BANK PERFORMANCE

    Quality at Entry
89. The project’s design reflected Government’s PFM reform priorities and benefited from input from PEFA
    assessments and lessons from the predecessor project. The project’s components captured many of the key
    reform areas and were broad in scope covering most elements of the annual budget cycle.
90. The extensive number of sub-components meant that there were many stakeholders required to be coordinated
    and activities to manage, it ended up being overly ambitious and the project was restructured mid-term. The
    project was designed with five components and twenty-five sub-components, involve PFM at the central and local
    government level and involved many implementing partners. The government’s capacity to implement the project
    as designed was not sufficient and this was reflected early with implementation delays with some areas not
    progressing at all.

91. The project did not appear to address an important set of binding constraints in the functioning of the PFM
    system through project design, which may have contributed to more impact. There are a number of known areas
    that constrain the PFM environment in Sierra Leone that could have been factored into the project’s design, some
    of which were recognized in the PPAR of the predecessor project20. These include:

        a. The existence of extra-budgetary funds that are not subject to parliamentary scrutiny (including for example
           the use of donor funds).

        b. Supplementary budgets that are not submitted to Parliament and include variations on approved budgets.

        c. Centralized payment approval processes that lead to inefficiencies.

        d. Connectivity issues that limit the likelihood that citizens can access information published on websites and
           open data portals.

        e. SAI dependence on the MoF for adequate funding.

        f.   Weak risk management and control environment at MDAs and local councils

        g. Low buy-in for an enhanced role for audit committees and enhanced PAC scrutiny


92. The results framework could have been more focused in areas of budget reliability, management of assets and
    liabilities, and the effectiveness of accountability institutions. The results framework tended to capture
    performance indicators and targets that did not reflect the change needed to improve the key issues in PFM in
    Sierra Leone. For example, although budget inputs were well covered there was little focus on the reliability of the
    budget. Also, the key area of public investment management was not reflected in the results framework at any
    stage despite the perennial poor performance in this area and the importance of it in supporting the country’s
    national development plan. Lastly, the establishment of audit committees at MDAs and their regular meetings did

    Quality of Supervision
94. Regular implementation support missions were undertaken throughout the project, and the issues identified
    were promptly addressed and adequately reported in aide memoires, ISRs and management letters. The
    frequency and quality of supervision appeared to be satisfactory. The average annual supervision cost was US$
    230,510 and an average of 29.4 staff weeks per year. The Aide Memoires and Management Letters captured
    implementation issues and set out comprehensively the issues and decisions taken, and the action plan agreed
    (which was extensively followed-up in the following missions). The Bank responded quickly and effectively to issues
    that arose. For the 2020 and 2021 project supervision was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and resulting
    restrictions of travel, however, this did not appear to have much negative impact and the virtual missions
    undertaken were effective in monitoring progress and addressing implementation issues. The task team needs to be
    credited for undertaking missions during the Ebola crisis and undertaking effective remote supervision during the
    COVID-19 travel ban and successfully closing the project virtually.


    Justification of Overall Rating of Bank Performance
95. The overall rating for the Bank’s performance is “Moderately Satisfactory”. Overall, the Bank team persistently
    addressed challenges that arose through preparation and implementation and provided useful technical support
    and proactive support in a timely manner. The quality on entry was hampered by designing a project that appeared
    overly ambitious in the context of the Government’s capacity to implement.

V. LESSONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS

          PFM Related

      99. PFM reform is broad scope, with many interdependent technical elements, many key stakeholders
          across levels of government and sectors. Coordinating PFM reform is a significant challenge given
          its technical complexities and vested interests. It is essential that there is a PFM reform strategy
          that is cohesive and with which all stakeholders are fully engaged. Having committed focal points
          on the Government side is essential as is well structured governance systems that oversee reforms
          with instituted effective feedback loops such that they can address issues quickly and adjust their
          strategy and/or its sequencing where required based on evolving circumstances. Having citizens
          engaged in PFM reform is also critical so that they can hold Government accountable for achieving
          the plan and offer technical advice. The more ‘strategic’ aspects PFM Reform including reform
          governance, monitoring progress dynamically in real-time, and engaging citizen groups as part of the
          reform process, as distinct from their involvement in the PFM system, requires capacity
          enhancements and can be intentionally factored into PFM project design.

      100. PFM reforms are a long-term undertaking with incremental gains even with more modern IT
         technologies to support improvements. Despite the modernization of technologies that can
         support PFM systems, implementing such technologies is very challenging especially in fragile
         contexts. Ensuring that systems can be seamlessly introduced into IT environments, having a clear
         description of ‘business processes’ and, therefore, requirements, training users to be able to benefit
         from the full functionality of the system, maintaining the IT environment and user support post-
         implementation to continue utilization and ensuring stakeholders (e.g., external auditors), also build
         capacity to undertake their role effectively in the PFM system (e.g., computer audits). Therefore, in
         addition to the ‘normal’ complexity of PFM reforms, including IT system implementation and roll-out
         into a PFM project requires careful planning, effective governance structures, effective coordination
         between entities and a clear ‘champion’ within Government.

      101. Ambitious projects that involve a wide range of highly technical areas, as is the case with PFM,
         require a careful assessment of the Government’s capacity to implement the project and a
         responsive and ongoing plan for comprehensive strategic, technical and project implementation
         TA support. The PFMICP project was very ambitious with many complex elements. Attempting to
         address significant capacity constraints during implementation can increase the risk of delays and
         non-achievement of the development objective. Some balance is needed between sequencing
         activities to align with capacity developing and building capacity in parallel with project
         implementation. Further balance is required to be more strategic in selecting activities that have

     highest impact and a realistic view of Government’s capacity to achieve such activities in a
     reasonable timeframe. It is also important when using PEFA indicators to have supplementary
     indicators that measure targeted performance areas that are linked to the assessed constraints to
     achieving the key results, this provides a clear focus on linking improving capacity to achieving
     results.

 102. Constraints in addressing the resolution of audit issues at MDAs should have been addressed
    more extensively with more of a focus early on and should not have been left unaddressed at the
    end of the project. With the passing of the PFM Act 2016 and associated 2018 regulations the PFM
    legal framework is reasonable, however, the capacity to implement the requirements is varied. In
    addition to building capacities to comply it is important to ensure that appropriate incentives are
    reflected to ensure MDAs comply, such as by including sanctions for no-compliance. Additionally,
    while the legal framework is adequate the coordination required by accountability institutions to
    follow-up on reported issues is lacking and the DPF operation in Sierra Leone is currently aiming to
    support this.

 103. The PEFA supplemental assessment on gender response public financial management
    demonstrated that there is a significant opportunity for PFM reform in Sierra Leone to be more
    gender responsive. The PEFA supplemental assessment in Sierra Leone has highlighted
    opportunities for Government to make the PFM process more gender responsive, this should be
    considered for further PFM reform initiatives and Bank PFM projects.

     General

 104. Continued focus is required to address procurement and contract management challenges on
    development projects in Sierra Leone. Procurement delays and lapses and contract management
    issues caused significant delays in project implementation, it is important that the Bank maintains its
    dialogue with the Government on how to address these constraints and issues.

 105. Attention to effective co-financier and donor coordination is important to avoid funding gaps
    and fragmented support to Government to implement its reforms. It is essential that donor
    collaboration and coordination is effective to gain synergies and maximize development support to
    Government. This includes having a collective understanding of reform priorities and shared
    expectations about what the project can achieve. The EU and FCDO withdrew from the PFMICP
    project early in project implementation, due to a change in their priorities, and this created a
    funding gap that was required to be filled and led to parallel donor activities in the PFM sphere.

 106. There should have been a plan to mainstream institutional recurrent costs, financed under the
    project, into the annual budget so that activities could be sustained after the project. The project
    financed the costs of the PAC Secretariat which was immediately decommissioned at the project’s
    close. Similarly, the project financed a Non-State Actors Secretariat which accomplished some good
    results such as the 2018 citizens budget and the management of the NSA Grants, once the funding
    stopped the NSA Secretariat was dissolved. The operation of the audit committees of the initial 15
    reconstituted was also financed under the project and it is essential that their funding is retained
